perturbation.original_text: 4.4 AUDIT RIGHTS. Corio shall keep true and accurate books of accounts and records for determining the amounts payable to Changepoint under this Agreement. Such books and records shall be kept for at least three (3) years following the end of the calendar month to which they pertain, and shall be open for inspection by an independent certified public accountant reasonably acceptable to Corio, and made subject to Corio's standard non-disclosure agreement, for the sole purpose of verifying the amounts payable to Changepoint under this Agreement. Such inspections may be made no more than once each calendar year, at reasonable times and upon reasonable notice. Changepoint shall bear all costs and expenses of such inspection. If any such inspection discloses a shortfall or an overpayment, the appropriate party shall promptly pay the amount of such shortfall or refund such overpayment. In addition, if any such inspection reveals an underpayment of more than five percent (5%) for the period under audit, Corio shall reimburse Changepoint for the reasonable cost of the examination.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.4 AUDIT RIGHTS. Corio shall keep books of accounts and records for determining the amounts payable to Changepoint under this Agreement. Such books and records shall be kept at Corio's discretion, and may be open for inspection at mutually agreed times. Each party shall bear their own costs and expenses of any inspection. If any inspection discloses a shortfall or an overpayment, the appropriate party may consider an adjustment.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the mandatory retention period (three years), limiting access to 'mutually agreed times,' and removing the requirement for a certified public accountant, the modified clause significantly weakens Changepoint's ability to audit Corio's records effectively. By removing the clause about an underpayment of 5% or more requiring reimbursement for the examination, this introduces further legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S. Code § 7262 mandates internal controls including accurate record keeping for publicly traded companies, to ensure financial transparency. The altered clause weakens audit rights to an extent that it may hinder compliance with regulations requiring accurate financial reporting and audit trails. Since Corio is providing services to Changepoint, where revenues are generated, this could be seen as circumventing SOX compliance by limiting accurate tracking of revenues generated from their partnership.

perturbation.justification: The altered contract weakens audit rights, potentially hindering compliance with regulations requiring accurate financial reporting. The original text mandates specific record-keeping and inspection protocols. The changed text introduces discretion in record-keeping ('at Corio's discretion') and inspection times ('at mutually agreed times'), and removes the requirement for an independent auditor. This contradicts the intention of 15 U.S. Code § 7262, as described in the Law Explanation, which mandates internal controls including accurate record keeping for publicly traded companies to ensure financial transparency. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 15 USC 7262 relating to management assessment of internal controls and requiring rules for annual reports to contain an internal control report. The weakened audit rights make it more difficult to establish and maintain adequate internal control structure, creating a legal contradiction as the altered clause conflicts with the purpose of the law.

perturbation.original_text: 14.3 CHOICE OF LAW; ARBITRATION; VENUE. This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California, USA, excluding conflict of laws provisions and excluding the 1980 United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods. Any disputes arising out of this Agreement shall be resolved by binding arbitration in accordance with the then-current commercial arbitration rules of the American Arbitration Association ("RULES"). The arbitration shall be conducted by one (1) arbitrator appointed in accordance with the Rules in San Francisco County, California. A judgment upon the award may be entered in any court having jurisdiction of the parties, including without limitation the courts in San Francisco, California. The non-prevailing party in the arbitration shall pay all fees and charges of the American Arbitration Association; each party, however, shall be responsible for the payment of all fees and expenses connected with the presentation of its respective case.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.3 CHOICE OF LAW; ARBITRATION; VENUE. This Agreement shall be subject to the laws determined at the company's discretion. Any disputes arising out of this Agreement shall be resolved through a method selected by Changepoint. Each party shall be responsible for the payment of all fees and expenses connected with the presentation of its respective case.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific reference to California law and arbitration rules, and instead deferring to methods selected by Changepoint, this clause creates an imbalance of power. This introduces legal risk because it eliminates the standard protections afforded by established legal frameworks and potentially violates principles of fair dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281 states that written agreements to arbitrate a controversy are valid and enforceable. The modified text contradicts this by removing the binding arbitration clause, giving one party (Changepoint) unilateral control over dispute resolution, and potentially undermining the enforceability of the arbitration agreement, violating established arbitration principles in California.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 2. Enforcement of Arbitration Agreements [1281 - 1281.99] ( Chapter 2 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1281. A written agreement to submit to arbitration an existing controversy or a controversy thereafter arising is valid, enforceable and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist for the revocation of any contract. (Repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461.)
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that disputes will be resolved by binding arbitration in accordance with the American Arbitration Association rules, governed by California law. The modified text removes these specific protections and instead states disputes will be resolved through a method selected by Changepoint and subject to the laws determined at the company's discretion, creating an imbalance of power. The scraped law snippet from California Code of Civil Procedure § 1281 states, "A written agreement to submit to arbitration an existing controversy or a controversy thereafter arising is valid, enforceable and irrevocable." By removing the arbitration clause and giving Changepoint unilateral control, the modified text contradicts the enforceability of arbitration agreements, which are legally protected under California law.

perturbation.original_text: 10 CONFIDENTIALITY. Each party hereby agrees that it shall not use any Confidential Information received from the other party other than as expressly permitted under the terms of the non-disclosure agreement in effect between the parties dated October 29, 1999, which is incorporated herein by reference and made a part hereof. For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Information" means the definition given to that term in such non-disclosure agreement. The parties agree that Changepoint's Software and Documentation (except that Documentation which this Agreement contemplates will be provided to Customers) shall be deemed Confidential Information subject to that non-disclosure agreement. The terms and conditions of this Agreement shall also be deemed Confidential Information subject to that non-disclosure agreement, but the terms of this Agreement may be disclosed by a party in connection with a significant transaction involving the merger or sale of all or a substantial part of the assets of the receiving party. Further, each party represents and warrants that their respective employees, agents, contractors or consultants that will be provided the other party's confidential information have or will have signed agreements with customary terms containing confidentiality provisions and assignment of inventions ("EMPLOYEE NDA/INVENTION AGREEMENT"). Each party covenants that during the term of this Agreement, it will continue to require all of such employees, agents, contractors or consultants to sign an Employee NDA/Invention Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10 CONFIDENTIALITY. Each party agrees it will make reasonable efforts to protect Confidential Information. For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Information" shall be limited to trade secrets. Each party will ensure its employees have been made aware of the need to protect confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit reference to and incorporation of the existing non-disclosure agreement, limiting the definition of confidential information only to "trade secrets", and weakening the requirement for signed employee NDA/invention agreements to a mere awareness notice, the modified clause drastically weakens the confidentiality protections. The NDA also includes the terms and conditions of the agreement, which are no longer protected under this clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426 defines trade secrets and the measures required to protect them. While the revised clause acknowledges the need to protect trade secrets, it removes the requirement for signed agreements with employees, weakening reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, potentially conflicting with CUTSA's requirements for preventing trade secret misappropriation and increasing the risk of information leaks.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens confidentiality protections by removing the incorporation of the existing non-disclosure agreement and limiting 'Confidential Information' to only 'trade secrets'. The original text ensures broader protection by referencing a specific NDA and including the agreement's terms. The omission of the explicit NDA and weakening of employee agreement requirements likely contradicts the reasonable measures expected to maintain trade secret protection, as explained in the Law Explanation, and thus creates a loophole regarding information beyond trade secrets, moving away from the original legally compliant position.
